cues.x: Shoe Dog, by Phil Knight.
Imagine a young man, twenty-four years old, up before the sun in Portland, Oregon, lacing up his running shoes and heading out into the cold morning fog.
It is nineteen sixty-two.
His legs carry him through the quiet streets, and while his body works, his mind runs even faster, because he is chasing something he cannot quite name yet.
He calls it the Crazy Idea.

And the thing you have to understand about Phil Knight is that this book, Shoe Dog, is the story of a man who bet his entire life on a hunch, who nearly went broke a dozen times chasing it, who was told over and over that he was foolish, and who kept going anyway.
It is the origin story of Nike, one of the most recognizable companies on the planet, but it is not told the way you would expect.
There is no gloss here, no victory lap.
Knight tells it raw.

He tells you about the fear, the debt, the sleepless nights, the friends who believed in him when the banks did not, and the strange emptiness he felt on the very night he became rich beyond imagining.
So settle in, because this is a story about obsession, about loyalty, about failing forward, and about what it really costs to build something that matters.
Let me take you back to the beginning, to where the Crazy Idea was born.
Phil Knight grew up in Oregon, a shy, competitive kid who ran track at the University of Oregon under a legendary and slightly terrifying coach named Bill Bowerman.
Running was the one place Knight felt fully himself.
After Oregon, he did a stint in the Army, and then he went to Stanford for his MBA.
And it was there, in a small business seminar, that everything changed.
The class assignment was to dream up a new business and write a paper laying out how you would launch it.
Most students picked something safe and forgettable.
Knight, who loved running and who had been quietly fascinated by Japan ever since the war, wrote about shoes.
Specifically, he wrote about running shoes.
His argument went like this.
Not long before, everyone assumed the best cameras in the world came from Germany, brands like Leica.
But then Japanese camera makers came along, made cameras that were just as good and far cheaper, and they ate the Germans alive.
Knight looked at the athletic shoe market and saw the exact same setup.
The premium running shoes of the day were German, brands like Adidas and Puma, expensive and dominant.
What if, Knight asked, Japanese factories could do to those German shoe brands exactly what Japanese camera makers had done to the German camera brands?
Make something just as good, sell it for less, and take over.
He wrote the paper, got a decent grade, and then, unlike almost everyone else who has ever written a school paper, he decided to actually go do it.
But first he wanted to see the world.
So in nineteen sixty-two, Knight went to his father, a respectable newspaper publisher, and asked for money to fund a trip around the globe.
His father, a conservative man who valued stability, surprised him by saying yes.
And so Knight set off, first with a college friend and then on his own, wandering through Hawaii, then Japan, then across Asia, the Middle East, Africa, and Europe.
This was not just a vacation.
It was a young man searching for meaning, wrestling with the big questions, reading philosophy, standing in ancient places and asking himself what he wanted his one life to be.
But woven through the whole trip was the Crazy Idea, waiting for its moment.
That moment came in Japan, in the city of Kobe.
Knight had researched Japanese shoe companies and settled on one called Onitsuka, the maker of a shoe brand called Tiger.
He wrangled a meeting with the executives, showed up in a suit, nervous as could be, a total unknown, an American kid with no company and no money and no track record.
The Onitsuka executives sat him down and, through the formal ritual of a Japanese business meeting, asked him a very reasonable question.
What company do you represent?
And Knight, caught completely off guard, his mind racing, blurted out the first thing that came to him.
Blue Ribbon Sports.
It was not a real company.
It did not exist.
He had invented it on the spot, drawing on the blue ribbons he had won as a kid in sports.
But the name landed, the executives nodded, and just like that Knight had, in a manner of speaking, a company.
He told them he wanted to distribute Tiger shoes in the western United States.
They agreed to send him samples.
He was elated.
The Crazy Idea had taken its first breath of real air.
There is a moment on that same world trip that Knight returns to, a moment that stayed with him for the rest of his life.
In Greece, he climbed to the Acropolis in Athens and stood before the temple of Athena Nike, the goddess of victory.
He did not know it then, of course, but that word, Nike, that idea of victory carved into ancient marble, was quietly planting itself in his mind, waiting years to bloom.
He felt something standing there, a sense of the ancient and the sacred and the enduring, and he carried that feeling home with him along with his samples of Japanese running shoes.
Back in Oregon, months passed, and finally the sample shoes arrived from Onitsuka.
Knight opened the box, held the Tigers in his hands, and felt certain.
He mailed two pairs to the one person in the world whose opinion mattered most to him: his old track coach, Bill Bowerman.
Now, Bowerman was not just any coach.
He was a genius and an eccentric, a man utterly obsessed with the idea that a lighter shoe could shave precious seconds off a runner's time.

He was forever tinkering, cutting shoes apart, gluing them back together, experimenting on his own athletes' feet, sometimes leaving them with blisters and bruises in his relentless pursuit of the perfect shoe.
Knight expected Bowerman to say the Tigers were nice.
Instead, Bowerman called him back with a proposal that stunned him.
Bowerman did not just want to buy shoes.
He wanted in.
He wanted to be Knight's partner, so he could get his hands directly on these Japanese shoes and start improving them.
They struck a deal, and it was a very Bowerman deal.
Bowerman insisted Knight take the controlling share.
Fifty-one percent for Knight, forty-nine percent for Bowerman.
The coach wanted Knight to be the boss, to have the final say.
And so Blue Ribbon Sports, the company that had been born as a nervous lie in a Kobe conference room, now had two partners, a few hundred dollars, and a big cardboard box of Japanese running shoes.
Now here is the part that people forget when they think about the birth of a giant company.
There was nothing glamorous about it.
Knight did not quit everything and go all in.
He was too practical, or maybe too scared, for that.
He got a job as an accountant, first at one firm and later teaching accounting at Portland State, because he needed the steady paycheck.
And on nights and weekends, he sold running shoes out of the trunk of his car, a green Plymouth Valiant.

He would drive up and down the Pacific Northwest to track meets, pop the trunk, and sell Tigers straight to runners and coaches who crowded around, because the shoes really were good and really were cheaper.
He sold out constantly.
The runners loved them.
Word spread.
And this is one of the quiet truths of the whole story: Knight succeeded first because he was selling to people like himself.
He was a runner selling to runners.
He believed in the product with the fervor of a true believer, and that belief was contagious.
He was not a natural salesman in the smooth, glad-handing sense.
He was shy, awkward, uncomfortable with pitching.
But he did not need to pitch.
He just needed to hand a serious runner a better, cheaper shoe and let the shoe do the talking.
As orders grew, Knight needed help, and that is when Jeff Johnson entered the story.

Johnson was Blue Ribbon's first full-time employee, and he was a character, an intense, zealous, endlessly enthusiastic man who threw himself into selling shoes with a passion that sometimes bordered on the fanatical.
Johnson kept obsessive files on his customers, index cards recording each runner's shoe size, their preferences, their injuries, their race times, their birthdays.
He wrote them long, heartfelt letters.
He essentially invented a kind of grassroots customer devotion before anyone had a word for it.
Johnson opened the first real Blue Ribbon retail store, a scrappy little place, and he lived and breathed the mission in a way that sometimes unnerved even Knight.

Johnson would write Knight letter after letter, pouring out ideas, asking for guidance, sometimes desperate for a reply, and Knight, being Knight, would often just leave the letters unanswered, letting Johnson twist.
It became a running theme, Johnson's flood of passionate communication and Knight's stony silence.
But Johnson was indispensable, one of the true believers, one of the misfits who would build this thing.
And I want you to hold onto that word, misfits, because it matters.
Knight did not build Nike with polished MBAs and slick executives.
He built it with oddballs and outcasts, fat guys, disabled guys, guys who did not fit in anywhere else, guys who found in this strange little shoe company a home and a purpose.
Now we come to the great engine of dread that drives the entire middle of this book, and it is not a villain or a rival.
It is cash flow.
To understand Phil Knight, you have to understand his philosophy of growth, which was ruthless and, frankly, a little insane.
His view was simple: grow or die.
Every single dollar the company made, he immediately poured back into ordering more shoes.
He never sat on a cushion of cash.
He never played it safe.

If he sold a thousand pairs this season, he would order two thousand for the next, then three thousand after that, doubling and doubling, betting everything on the next order being bigger than the last.
And this meant that Blue Ribbon, even as its sales soared year after year, was perpetually, terrifyingly broke.
On paper the company was a rocket ship.
In the bank account it was always a hair away from zero.
Knight was constantly scrambling to make payments to Onitsuka in Japan, constantly waiting for shoes to arrive and sell so he could pay for the shoes he had already ordered.
To finance this, Knight needed a bank, and here he ran headlong into the single most maddening relationship of his early career.
His bank, First National Bank of Oregon, and the bankers there, could not comprehend what he was doing.
Bankers, by nature, love cash reserves.
They love to see a company sitting on a comfortable pile of money, growing slowly and safely.
Knight was the exact opposite.
He had no reserves, ever, on purpose, because every dollar was working.
His banker would look at the books, see rapid growth but no cash cushion, and grow more and more alarmed.
The banker kept telling him to slow down, to build up equity, to stop growing so fast.
And Knight kept ignoring him, because slowing down felt like death.
This tension built and built until First National finally did the thing Knight feared most.
They cut him off.
They told him they would no longer extend credit, and they essentially showed him the door.
For a company that ran entirely on borrowed money and rapid reinvestment, losing your bank was potentially fatal.
Knight was left stranded, needing to pay the Japanese for a huge shipment of shoes, with no line of credit and no obvious way to survive.
And this is where a savior appeared, from an unexpected corner of the world.
There was a Japanese trading company called Nissho Iwai, one of those enormous, ancient Japanese trading houses that financed commerce across the globe.
Through his dealings in Japan, Knight connected with Nissho, and they agreed to help finance his shoe imports.
Nissho essentially stepped in where the American bank had abandoned him, fronting the money to pay for the Japanese shoes and taking their cut.
There was a man at Nissho, and others over the years, who believed in Knight and in Blue Ribbon, who saw the growth and the potential where the stodgy American bankers saw only risk.
Nissho became Blue Ribbon's lifeline, its secret weapon, the thing that let Knight keep growing when the traditional financial world had turned its back.
Time and again, when it looked like the whole enterprise would collapse under the weight of its own ambition, Nissho was there.
Keep that relationship in mind, because it is going to come back and save Knight's life in the most dramatic moment of the entire story.
Meanwhile, back in Oregon, Bill Bowerman was doing what Bowerman did best: obsessing over the shoe itself.
And this leads to one of the most beloved legends in all of business, the birth of the waffle sole.
Bowerman was forever hunting for a better traction, a better grip, a sole that would give runners more spring and speed without adding weight.
One Sunday morning, sitting at the breakfast table with his wife, he stared at the family waffle iron, at that grid of little squares, and something clicked in his mind.
What if a shoe sole had that same waffle pattern of nubs?
So Bowerman, being Bowerman, grabbed a container of urethane, the rubbery liquid he had been experimenting with, and poured it right into the family waffle iron.
He ruined the waffle iron, of course.
The urethane welded the thing shut.

But out of that reckless kitchen experiment came the idea for a revolutionary running sole, a pattern of small rubber studs that gripped the track and gave a bouncy, lightweight ride that runners had never felt before.
The waffle sole became one of the defining innovations of the young company, a genuine leap forward born not in a lab but at a breakfast table, out of one obsessive man's refusal to stop tinkering.
It is the perfect symbol of the whole spirit of the place: homemade, a little reckless, driven by love of the craft, willing to destroy a perfectly good waffle iron in pursuit of something better.
So the shoes were getting better, sales were growing, the team of misfits was assembling, and Nissho was keeping the money flowing.
But storm clouds were gathering over the most important relationship of all, the one with Onitsuka in Japan, the company whose Tiger shoes were the only product Blue Ribbon had to sell.
And here enters a man named Kitami, an executive at Onitsuka.
At first Kitami seemed like an ally, a rising star at the Japanese company who dealt with Knight directly.
But over time Knight began to sense that something was very wrong.
Onitsuka was slow to ship, was flirting with other American distributors, was treating Blue Ribbon with less and less respect even as Blue Ribbon's sales climbed.
The terrible vulnerability of Knight's position became clear.
His entire company depended on a single supplier, and that supplier could destroy him at any moment simply by cutting him off.
Blue Ribbon had no product of its own.
It was, at bottom, just a middleman for Japanese shoes.
The betrayal, when it came, was worse than Knight had feared.
During a meeting, Kitami left a folder or a briefcase unattended, and Knight, or one of his people, got a look at what was inside.
And there it was in black and white: a list.
Onitsuka had drawn up a secret list of other American distributors, companies they were planning to hand the business to, companies that would replace Blue Ribbon.

Kitami was quietly preparing to cut Knight out entirely and give the American market to someone else, after all the years Knight had spent building demand for Tiger shoes from the trunk of his Plymouth.
It was a gut punch.
The company Knight had bet his life on was about to have its one and only product yanked away.
Now, a lesser man might have folded, might have begged Onitsuka to keep him on, might have accepted whatever scraps they offered.
Knight did something far braver and far more terrifying.
He decided that if Onitsuka was going to abandon him, then Blue Ribbon would stop being a distributor of someone else's shoes and become a maker of its own.
He would launch his own brand.
He had already quietly started arranging to manufacture shoes at other factories, including in Japan and eventually elsewhere, hedging against exactly this betrayal.
But to fully break free, he needed his own brand, his own name, his own identity.
And he needed it fast, because the shoes were about to start rolling off the production line and they had to have a name and a logo stamped on them.
This is the famous eleventh-hour scramble, and it is almost comic how close to the wire it went.
The company gathered to decide on a name for their new brand, and the deadline was brutal.
The factory needed the name immediately.
People threw out ideas, most of them terrible.
Knight himself was partial to a name that, in hindsight, would have been a disaster.
He liked Dimension Six.
Just sit with how different the world would be if the greatest athletes on earth wore Dimension Six.
Nobody loved the options.
And then, at the last possible moment, Jeff Johnson, that same zealous first employee, came forward with a name that had come to him.
He said the word had appeared to him in a dream, or half-dream, when he woke in the early morning.
The name was Nike.
Nike, the ancient Greek goddess of victory, the very goddess whose temple Phil Knight had stood before all those years ago on the Acropolis in Athens.
Knight did not love it at first.
Nobody in the room was fully convinced.
But time had run out, and Nike was the least bad option, and it had that one strange thread of destiny running through it, that echo of the temple of Athena Nike.
So they went with it.
They chose victory, almost by accident, almost against their own instincts, on a deadline, at the very last minute.
And then there was the logo, and this is maybe the most famous bargain in the history of branding.
Knight needed a design, a mark, something to stamp on the side of the shoe.
He turned to a young graphic design student he had met while teaching at Portland State, a woman named Carolyn Davidson.
He hired her to come up with something, something that suggested motion, that would look good on the side of a shoe.
She sketched and sketched, and eventually produced a simple, curved, checkmark-like shape, a fluid little wing of a thing that would come to be called the Swoosh.
She presented several options, and the team gathered around, and once again nobody was blown away.
They picked the Swoosh mostly by process of elimination, because the deadline was on top of them and it was the best of the bunch.
Knight looked at it and delivered what may be the most gloriously understated line in business history.
He said something to the effect of, I don't love it, but it'll grow on me.
And for that logo, that Swoosh that is now worth untold billions and is recognized in every corner of the earth, Carolyn Davidson was paid thirty-five dollars.
Thirty-five dollars for the Swoosh.
Knight, to his credit, never forgot it, and years later, after the company had become an empire, he made sure to give her stock and recognition worthy of what she had created.
But in that moment, on that deadline, it was just thirty-five dollars for a logo nobody was even sure they liked.
So Nike was born, the goddess of victory with the Swoosh on her side, and Blue Ribbon began selling its own shoes.
But breaking away from Onitsuka did not come clean.
It came with a lawsuit.
Onitsuka and Blue Ribbon ended up in court, each side accusing the other of breaking their agreement, fighting over contracts and territory and who had wronged whom.
For Knight, a lawsuit was another kind of terror, more money bleeding out, more uncertainty, the fate of his company placed in the hands of lawyers and a judge.
The trial was tense and dramatic, with testimony and accusations flying.
But in the end, Blue Ribbon prevailed.
They won damages, they won their freedom, and they came out the other side as a fully independent company with its own brand and its own future.
The break with the Japanese supplier that had seemed like it might kill the company instead set it free.
Now I want to slow down and talk about a person, because the soul of a company is never its balance sheet.
The soul of early Nike was a runner named Steve Prefontaine.
If you know anything about American distance running, you know that name.
Pre, as everyone called him, was a phenomenon, a runner from Oregon, coached by Bowerman, who ran with a ferocity and a fearlessness that made him a genuine sports hero.
He did not run to conserve energy or to win cautiously.
He ran from the front, all out, daring anyone to keep up, treating every race like a test of guts.
He held American records, he electrified crowds, he was cocky and charismatic and utterly beloved.
And he became the face and the heart of Nike.
He wore the shoes, he believed in the mission, he fought alongside Knight against the establishment of amateur athletics that Pre felt was exploiting runners.
For Knight, who had been a runner himself, who had never been anywhere near as good as Pre, having this magnificent athlete carry the Nike banner was almost a religious experience.
Pre embodied everything the company stood for: rebellion, heart, the refusal to hold anything back.
And then came the tragedy that hangs over the whole book like a shadow.
Steve Prefontaine died in a car accident.
He was twenty-four years old.
He crashed his car late one night after a race, after a party, and he was gone, just like that, in the fullness of his youth and his talent, with his greatest races still ahead of him.
The loss devastated Knight and everyone at Nike and the entire running world.
Pre was more than an endorser or an athlete.
He was one of them, one of the family, the living, breathing embodiment of the company's spirit.
Knight writes about it with real grief.
Pre's death was a wound that never fully closed, and the company kept his memory sacred for decades afterward.
It is a reminder, right in the middle of this business story, that the things we build are made of people, and people are fragile, and the ones who matter most can be taken from us far too soon.
Even as the company mourned and grew and found its footing as an independent brand, the financial terror never really went away.
Remember Knight's grow-or-die philosophy?
He never stopped.
The bigger Nike got, the bigger the orders, the bigger the debt, the bigger the risk.
And this brings us to the single most frightening crisis of the whole saga, the one in nineteen seventy-five that came within a whisker of ending everything.

Nike had grown so fast, and its financing was so stretched and so unconventional, running through Nissho and the banks in a complicated web, that at one point the cash simply was not there to cover what was owed.
There was a shortfall, a gap between money going out and money coming in, and it triggered an absolute catastrophe.
The bank, alarmed by what it saw in Nike's accounts, by checks and balances that did not seem to add up, did something drastic.
They froze the company's accounts.
Overnight, Nike could not access its own money.
And then it got worse, far worse.
The bank, suspecting that something criminal might be going on, that this might be fraud, alerted the FBI.
Suddenly Phil Knight, who had done nothing but chase his Crazy Idea and reinvest every dollar as fast as he could, was facing not just bankruptcy but the possibility of a federal fraud investigation.
It was the nightmare scenario, the whole thing collapsing, his reputation and possibly his freedom on the line.
And once again, in the darkest hour, salvation came from Japan.
Nissho, the great trading company that had been Knight's lifeline for years, stepped in and covered the gap.
They put up the money, they vouched for Nike, they essentially rescued the company from the abyss.

There is a moment where Knight realizes just how much Nissho had quietly done for him over the years, how they had believed in him and backed him when his own countrymen's banks would not, and it is genuinely moving.
Nike survived nineteen seventy-five not because of clever American finance but because of the loyalty and vision of a Japanese trading house that saw what the company could become.

After that near-death experience, Knight finally began to build the more solid financial footing that the company needed, but he never forgot how close it had come, and he never forgot who had saved him.
Now let me bring you back to those misfits, because the people around Knight are the beating heart of this whole story, and I do not want to rush past them.
There was Bob Woodell.

Woodell had been a promising young athlete at Oregon, a track and field competitor, until a terrible accident during a celebration left him paralyzed, confined to a wheelchair for the rest of his life.
Knight brought Woodell into the company, and Woodell turned out to be one of the most capable, steady, and beloved people in the whole operation.

He ran operations, he managed the endless logistical chaos of getting shoes made and shipped and sold, and he did it all from his wheelchair, with a competence and good humor that anchored the company through its most turbulent years.
Knight and Woodell developed a deep, wordless friendship, two men who did not need to say much to understand each other.
And there is a detail about Woodell that will break your heart and lift it at the same time.
At one of the many points when Knight was desperate for cash, when the company was teetering, Woodell's parents, ordinary working people, lent Knight their life savings.
Their entire savings, handed over to keep this shaky little shoe company alive, because their son believed in it and because they believed in their son.
That is the kind of faith and loyalty this company ran on.
Not venture capital, not slick investors, but the life savings of an employee's parents, given in trust.

There was Rob Strasser, a big, brilliant, combative lawyer and strategist who became one of Knight's key lieutenants, a man of huge appetites and huge ideas who threw himself into battles on Nike's behalf.
There were the others too, the early accountants and salesmen and oddballs, the men Knight affectionately grouped together as his band of misfits.
And then there was Penny.
Penny was one of Knight's accounting students at Portland State, then she came to work for Blue Ribbon as his bookkeeper, keeping the shaky finances in some kind of order.
Knight fell in love with her, and she with him, and they married.

Penny went from being the woman who kept the company's books to being Knight's wife and partner and the anchor of his personal life, raising their sons while Phil poured himself, often too much of himself, into the company.

Their marriage weathered the enormous strain of building Nike, the money troubles, the travel, the obsession, and Penny remained one of the constants in a life that was otherwise a whirlwind of risk and uncertainty.
And here I have to tell you about the Buttfaces, because it is one of the most delightful things in the whole book.
As Nike grew, Knight started holding regular retreats where his top people, his misfits and lieutenants, would gather to argue, strategize, drink, and hash out the future of the company.
These meetings were loud, profane, brutally honest, and often hilarious.
There was no corporate politeness, no polished presentation decorum.
People screamed at each other, insulted each other mercilessly, and then laughed about it.
And out of this raucous culture came a name they gave themselves, a name that captured perfectly their self-deprecating, anti-corporate spirit.
They called their gatherings the Buttface meetings, and they called themselves the Buttfaces.
It started as a joke, a bit of crude affection, someone hollering the word across the room, and it stuck.
These Buttface retreats were where the real decisions got made, where the loyalty was forged, where a group of misfits who did not fit anywhere else became a family and a fighting force.
It tells you everything about the culture Knight built.
It was irreverent, fiercely loyal, allergic to pretension, and bonded by a shared sense of being underdogs and outsiders taking on the giants of the world.
Through the late nineteen seventies, Nike kept growing, kept fighting, kept innovating.
There were battles with the government over import duties that nearly crippled the company with a massive, retroactive tax bill.
There were battles with Adidas, the German giant Knight had dreamed of toppling back in that Stanford paper.
There was the constant race to design better shoes, to sign better athletes, to expand into new markets.
The company that had started with a lie in a Kobe conference room and shoes sold from a car trunk was now a real force, a genuine challenger to the established order of the athletic world.
The Crazy Idea was coming true, right there in the numbers, right there on the feet of runners across America and beyond.

And that brings us to December of nineteen eighty, and to the moment the whole book has been building toward, the moment that transformed Phil Knight from a perpetually broke, perpetually anxious entrepreneur into one of the wealthiest men in America.
Nike went public.

After all those years of scrambling for cash, of being cut off by banks, of leaning on Nissho, of pouring every dollar back into the next order, Knight took the company public, offering its shares on the stock market.
And in doing so, the value of Knight's own stake in the company he had built was suddenly, officially, put down on paper for all the world to see.
On the day of the IPO, Phil Knight was worth roughly one hundred and seventy-eight million dollars.
Overnight.

A man who had spent nearly two decades one bad month away from ruin was now, by any measure, rich beyond anything he could have imagined when he was selling Tigers out of the trunk of his green Plymouth Valiant.
And here is what makes Phil Knight, and this book, so honest and so human.
That night, the night he became worth a hundred and seventy-eight million dollars, he did not feel triumphant.
He did not feel like celebrating.
He describes feeling strangely, profoundly empty.
He and Penny went out to dinner, and the moment was oddly flat, anticlimactic, hollow.
All those years of striving, all that fear and sacrifice and obsession, and the great payoff arrived and did not feel like anything at all.

It is one of the most quietly devastating passages in the book, because it captures a truth that our culture almost never admits out loud: that the money was never really the point, and reaching the destination you have chased your whole life can leave you feeling curiously untethered, wondering what it was all for and what comes next.
The victory was real, but victory, it turns out, does not fill the hole that the striving filled.
The chase had been the thing.
The chase had been the meaning.
In the closing pages, Knight, an older man now, looks back over the whole sweep of his life and his company, and he reflects with real wisdom and real regret.

Nike would go on, of course, to become the colossus we know today, the Swoosh on the feet of Michael Jordan and countless others, the Just Do It slogan echoing around the world, one of the most valuable brands ever built.
But Knight does not dwell on the glory.
He dwells on the cost, and on the meaning.
And his regrets are not about business decisions.
He does not lie awake wishing he had made a different deal or launched a different shoe.
His deepest regret is about time, the time he did not spend with his family, particularly with his sons, because he was so consumed by the company.
He built Nike, but he was often absent from his own home, present in body but lost in the endless problems of the business.
And he suffered a father's worst grief along the way.
He writes with unbearable tenderness and pain about his son Matthew, who as an adult died in a scuba diving accident, another young life lost too soon, another wound that never healed.
The man who won everything the world can offer still carries the losses that no amount of money can touch.
So what does the story of Shoe Dog finally teach us?
Knight leaves us with a message that is far deeper than any business lesson, and I want to give it to you the way he gives it.
First, he says, chase a calling, not just a job.
He begs the young especially not to settle for a paycheck, not to sleepwalk through a career that means nothing to them.

Find the thing you were made to do, the thing that lights you up the way running and shoes lit him up, and then pursue it with everything you have, even when everyone tells you it is crazy, even when the banks cut you off, even when the odds say you will fail.
A calling will carry you through the fear that a mere job never could.
Second, he says, give the world the best of yourself.
Do not hold back, do not coast, do not save your best self for some someday that never comes.
Pour yourself out.
Prefontaine ran that way, from the front, holding nothing in reserve, and though it may have cost him, it made him immortal.
Knight built Nike that way, betting everything, reinvesting every dollar, refusing to play it safe, and though it nearly killed him a dozen times, it built something that outlasted him.
Third, and maybe most important, he says a business is not a cold machine for making money.
A business is a living thing.
It is made of people, of their loyalty and their sweat and their belief.

Nike was Jeff Johnson's index cards and Bill Bowerman's ruined waffle iron and Bob Woodell running operations from a wheelchair and his parents handing over their life savings and Penny keeping the books and the Buttfaces screaming at each other and Steve Prefontaine running his heart out and dying young.
It was made of heart.
And the people who make such a thing succeed are not the smartest or the richest or the best connected.

They are simply the ones stubborn enough to keep going, to endure one more crisis, to survive one more frozen bank account, to get up before dawn and lace up their shoes and keep running toward the Crazy Idea long after any reasonable person would have quit.
And here is the final, beautiful irony of the whole story.
Nike would one day give the world its most famous three words, Just Do It, a slogan that would inspire millions to stop making excuses and start moving.
But Phil Knight did not need the slogan.
He lived it long before it was ever printed on an ad.

From the moment he blurted out Blue Ribbon Sports in that conference room in Kobe, from the moment he chose to launch his own brand rather than beg for scraps, from every terrifying doubling-down and every night the money almost ran out, he was doing exactly that, just doing it, taking the leap without knowing if the ground would be there, acting in the face of fear because the calling demanded it.

Shoe Dog is the story of a shy accountant who loved to run and who refused to let a crazy idea die, and in refusing, changed the world, and in the end, learned that the running itself, the striving, the family of misfits, the chase, was the whole point all along.
So whatever your Crazy Idea is, the one you have been afraid to chase, let this story be the nudge you needed.
Lace up.
And just do it.
